meta.title: The Nations This Morning
meta.headline: Ottawa named a port expansion as a national priority; Tsawwassen First Nation answered by naming the conditions.

throughLine: The weekend did not produce a flood of new Indigenous economic files. It produced one hard test: Roberts Bank Terminal 2 is now being treated as a national export-capacity file, while Tsawwassen First Nation is reminding governments and proponents that consent, compensation, employment and the environmental record still sit in front of the economics.

oneThing.headline: Roberts Bank enters the fast-track conversation, with Tsawwassen setting conditions
paragraphs: Canada announced a Port of Vancouver Gateway Strategy on July 16 and identified Roberts Bank Terminal 2 as the first port project for review by the new Major Projects Office. The federal release says the strategy is meant to expand trade capacity through the gateway and double non-U.S. exports over the next decade.

Tsawwassen First Nation responded the same day. Its statement says the Nation has signed agreements with the Vancouver Fraser Port Authority and Global Container Terminals, but also says those agreements are not unconditional support for the project. Tsawwassen says its participation is tied to mitigation of environmental impacts, employment and business opportunities, compensation for impacts to treaty rights and continued involvement in project decisions.

That makes the economic file more precise. RBT2 is not only a port-capacity story or a permitting-speed story. It is a treaty-rights, compensation and local-benefit test inside one of Canada's most important export corridors.
oneThing.whyItMatters: The project sits at the junction of federal growth policy, supply-chain infrastructure and First Nations treaty rights. If the Major Projects Office is going to work, this is the kind of file where the commercial schedule and consent architecture have to be legible at the same time.
unknowns: The federal release does not say when the Major Projects Office review will finish or whether the project will be put on any formal fast-track list.
Tsawwassen First Nation's response does not disclose the economics of its agreements with the port authority or Global Container Terminals.
It remains unclear how Ottawa will reconcile export-speed targets with the conditions named by Tsawwassen First Nation and other affected rights holders.
oneThing.nextCatalyst: The next catalyst is the Major Projects Office process for RBT2, including any posted project list, review timeline, conditions or updated accommodation commitments.

developments.headline: Treaty No. 6 and Quebec First Nations push Alberta separation into the rights-risk ledger
developments.whatHappened: APTN reported that Quebec First Nations joined Treaty No. 6 and other Alberta First Nations organizations in condemning Premier Danielle Smith's referendum legislation and Alberta-separation rhetoric. The Confederacy of Treaty No. 6 First Nations had already said chiefs passed an emergency resolution at the AFN annual general assembly opposing any Alberta separatist movement and asserting that treaty rights were made with the Crown, not a province acting alone.
developments.whyItMatters: The direct economic issue is not a referendum date. It is uncertainty around land, resources, jurisdiction, Crown obligations and project approvals in a province central to Canada's energy and infrastructure economy. Treaty organizations are putting governments and proponents on notice that constitutional politics cannot be separated from the rights framework that major projects depend on.
developments.whatRemainsUnknown: No formal separation referendum has been called, and the final AFN resolution package was not visible during today's scan.
developments.nextCatalyst: Watch for the posted AFN resolution package, Alberta's referendum-regulation process and any formal response from Canada or Alberta to treaty-rights concerns.

developments.headline: Ontario wildfire response becomes an emergency-infrastructure file
developments.whatHappened: Canadian Press reporting carried by CityNews said Ontario is expanding use of Alert Ready so local authorities, including First Nations chiefs, can issue urgent public-safety alerts during emergencies. Separate weekend reporting described the evacuation of Namaygoosisagagun First Nation after wildfire destroyed most of the community site, while Public Safety Canada said Ottawa had approved Ontario's request for federal assistance for the 2026 wildfire season.
developments.whyItMatters: Emergency alerts, aircraft, housing, communications, roads and rebuild funding are public infrastructure under pressure. For remote First Nations, wildfire response can quickly become a capital-capacity file: the fiscal question is who funds evacuation, where people live afterward and how rebuilding is governed.
developments.whatRemainsUnknown: The public record does not yet settle rebuild funding for Namaygoosisagagun, the division of federal-provincial costs, or whether local alert authority will be matched by communications infrastructure in the highest-risk communities.
developments.nextCatalyst: Watch federal and Ontario disaster-assistance decisions, community rebuild updates and whether Alert Ready use changes evacuation timing for northern First Nations.

developments.headline: B.C.'s fresh signal is RBT2; older project-risk files stayed open
developments.whatHappened: The B.C. scan found one material new project signal: Roberts Bank Terminal 2 being pulled into Ottawa's Port of Vancouver Gateway Strategy and Major Projects Office review, with Tsawwassen First Nation's response setting clear conditions around environmental mitigation, jobs, business opportunities and treaty-rights impacts. No material new public filing was found on the Simpcw-Yellowhead assessment agreement, the B.C.-Gitanmaax cannabis agreement, Tsleil-Waututh Nation's Burrard Inlet dredging challenge or B.C.'s Mount Polley tailings-dam approval over Xat'sull First Nation concerns.
developments.whyItMatters: B.C.'s economic signal remains split between new agreements and unresolved risk ledgers. RBT2 raises national-trade stakes; Yellowhead, Gitanmaax, Burrard and Mount Polley remain live tests of whether consent, benefit, regulatory durability and project economics can move together.
developments.whatRemainsUnknown: Court schedules, permit steps, agreement implementation and Major Projects Office process details remain outstanding.
developments.nextCatalyst: RBT2 review details, Yellowhead assessment documents, Gitanmaax implementation, Burrard judicial-review filings and Mount Polley permit steps.

developments.headline: B.C. First Nations are looking at nuclear, but the model is still Ontario's
developments.whatHappened: Global News reported that a delegation of B.C. First Nations travelled to Ontario to learn more about nuclear energy and small modular reactors. The fresh B.C. item is exploratory, not a project proposal or financing. The verified model in the background remains Ontario's Darlington New Nuclear Project, where the province has described up to $1 billion of loan guarantees to support an equity opportunity for Williams Treaties First Nations and the federal Major Projects Office lists Darlington as one of its named projects.
developments.whyItMatters: Nuclear is moving from an abstract energy-transition debate into an Indigenous ownership and capital-access question. For B.C., the immediate signal is not that a project exists; it is that some First Nations are studying whether Ontario's equity-and-loan-guarantee structure can travel to another grid and another rights landscape.
developments.whatRemainsUnknown: The public reporting does not identify a B.C. host project, ownership proposal, regulatory path or participating Nations' final positions.
developments.nextCatalyst: Watch whether any B.C. Nation, utility, regulator or project proponent publishes a formal nuclear or SMR study, partnership or policy position.

whatHappensNext: RBT2: watch for the Major Projects Office process, any formal review timeline and updated accommodation commitments tied to Tsawwassen First Nation's conditions.
Alberta referendum file: watch for the AFN resolution package, Alberta's referendum rules and any Crown response to Treaty No. 6 concerns.
Wildfires: watch whether evacuation authority and federal assistance translate into rebuild funding, communications capacity and long-term housing plans.
B.C. ledger: keep Yellowhead, Gitanmaax, Burrard and Mount Polley open until a filing, permit, agreement detail or court step materially changes the file.
Nuclear: watch whether exploratory visits become a named B.C. project, policy process or Indigenous equity proposal.
stillWatching: This edition's material developments are First Nations-specific. Inuit and Métis sources were scanned, but no Inuit or Métis economic item cleared the weekend materiality and verification bar.
The LNG Canada Phase 2 storage-tank equity option remains unresolved; no new financing, final-investment-decision or lease-economics disclosure appeared in today's scan.
The federal clean-water and child-welfare fiscal-design files remain open after the AFN assembly, but no new public document materially changed either file over the weekend.
First Peoples Law's July 8-14 report remained the latest visible legal roundup during the scan; no new court decision was elevated into today's brief.
